column1:
An in-situ vein graft exam must include the evaluation of any patent branches because:

A: of the potential for AV fistula formation
B: of increased risk for the formation of an AV malformation
C: they are necessary for proper graft function
D: of the increased risk of stenosis in the branch
column2:
Answer: A. of the potential for AV fistula formation

Feedback:
In-situ and reversed vein grafts should have all branches ligated before being placed as a bypass conduit. This process is more effectively performed on the reversed vein graft because it is removed from the leg before it is placed as a graft. An AV fistula is the most common complication of an in-situ vein graft because the venous branches can still be visibly patent. The arterial flow entering the venous branches can cause an AV fistula. An AV malformation is a congenital connection between an artery and vein.

column1:
A 65yr old male presents with mild left calf pain after walking 10 blocks. The pain is relieved by sitting and resting. On a resting arterial exam with ABI, the left ABI is 1.04. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: An exercise arterial should be performed to re-evaluate the ABI of the left leg before a diagnosis is made.
B: The venous system should be evaluated for possible insufficiency.
C: The patient is most likely experiencing an orthopedic or neurogenic problem and an MRI may be warranted.
D: The venous system should be evaluated for thrombosis.
column2:
Answer: A. An exercise arterial should be performed to re-evaluate the ABI of the left leg before a diagnosis is made.

Feedback:
Because the symptoms occur when the patient is walking, the leg should be evaluated with exercise to completely rule out a vascular diagnosis.

column1:
According to the Appropriate Use Criteria, which of the following is an inappropriate indication for an upper extremity arterial duplex exam?

A: suspected positional arterial obstruction
B: unilateral cold hand
C: 1 month follow up baseline exam post-intervention
D: unilateral arm swelling
column2:
Answer: D. unilateral arm swelling

Feedback:
Limb swelling is a common symptom of extremity venous disease, not arterial disease.

column1:
Which of the following statements best describes the Doppler tracing found in the stalk of a pseudoaneurysm?

A: high resistance to-and-fro flow
B: phasic forward flow
C: low resistance to-and-fro flow
D: continuous forward flow
column2:
Answer: A. high resistance to-and-fro flow

Feedback:
The body of the pseudo aneurysm will demonstrate a low resistance "swirling" of flow on the Doppler tracing. The neck/stalk of the pseudo aneurysm is normally very narrow and will demonstrate a high resistance, biphasic waveform.

column1:
________________________ is a nonspecific sign of DVT. It is defined as pain experienced upon quick dorsiflexion of the foot.

A: Homan Sign
B: Asher Sign
C: Allen Sign
D: Henderson Sign
column2:
Answer: A. Homan Sign

Feedback:
Homan sign is a nonspecific sign of DVT. It is defined as pain experienced upon quick dorsiflexion of the foot. Asher sign refers to enlargement of the spleen that extends across the midline, anterior to the abdominal aorta. The Allen Test is used to determine ulnar artery patency related to graft availability for removing the radial artery.

column1:
According to the attached color map, what color will laminar flow be demonstrated that is moving at 40cm/s away from the probe?

A: yellow
B: green
C: blue
D: red
column2:
Answer: C. blue

Feedback:
The color on the bottom of the color bar indicates flow moving away from the transducer. The variance map allows for evaluation of turbulent flow. If a green color appears, the flow is moving away from the probe and is turbulent. Flow velocities greater than 0.45 m/s will alias causing display of yellow (turbulent) or red.

column1:
____________________ cause primary varicose veins, while ___________________ cause secondary varicose veins.

A: incompetent valves, elevated deep venous pressures
B: incompetent valves, incompetent perforators
C: elevated deep venous pressures, incompetent valves
D: elevated deep venous pressures, elevated arterial pressures
column2:
Answer: A. incompetent valves, elevated deep venous pressures

Feedback:
Incompetent valves cause primary varicose veins, while elevated deep venous pressures in a diseased deep system (hx of DVT or abnormal valves) cause secondary varicose veins. Primary varicose veins are much more common than secondary.

column1:
Which of the following changes in flow can be seen with a Baker cyst?

A: pulsatile flow in the superficial femoral vein
B: continuous flow in the popliteal artery
C: continuous flow in the popliteal vein
D: pulsatile flow in the popliteal vein
column2:
Answer: C. continuous flow in the popliteal vein

Feedback:
A Baker cyst can cause pain and swelling in the popliteal fossa. The extrinsic compression of the popliteal vein by the cyst can lead to a continuous flow pattern, blood stasis and DVT formation. The arterial walls are muscular and can prevent the effects of extrinsic compression on the popliteal artery.

column1:
A patient presents for an ABI exam due to bilateral calf pain. He is a dialysis patient with an occluded graft in the right arm and a patent graft in the left arm. What should you do first?

A: Skip the brachial pressures and just do the ankle pressures
B: Change the exam t o a lower extremity arterial exam and start scanning
C: Take the pressure in the arm with the occluded graft
D: Call the referring physician to explain that the exam is not possible for this patient
column2:
Answer: D. Call the referring physician to explain that the exam is not possible for this patient

Feedback:
NEVER take a blood pressure in an arm with a hemodialysis graft. The ABI exam requires accurate brachial pressure readings in order to calculate the ABI. Without the brachial pressures, the exam is non-diagnostic. You should contact the referring physician to alert them of the issue and ask if they would like to order a different exam to assist the patient. NEVER cancel or change a physician's order. They must submit a new order if something changes.

column1:
A patient presents with a history of HTN, diabetes and claudication. The arterial Doppler evaluation demonstrates monophasic flow in all calf vessels, but the ABI values are LT 1.36 and RT 1.32. Which of the following correctly describes the findings?

A: The exam is normal and additional testing should be performed to rule out extrinsic vessel compression due to median arcuate ligament syndrome.
B: The exam is normal and additional testing should be performed to rule out extrinsic vessel compression due to popliteal entrapment syndrome.
C: The most likely diagnosis is thromboangiitis obliterans, which leads to medial calcification of the calf vessels.
D: The ABI values are abnormally elevated due to a diabetic complication called medial calcification.
column2:
Answer: D. The ABI values are abnormally elevated due to a diabetic complication called medial calcification.

Feedback:
Monophasic flow in the extremities is normally associated with an ABI less than 0.9. Diabetes can lead to medial calcification of the arterial walls which will falsely elevate the ankle pressures.

column1:
Budd Chiari syndrome refers to acute or chronic obstruction of which vessel(s)?

A: Splenic veins
B: Splenic artery
C: Hepatic artery
D: Hepatic veins
column2:
Answer: D. Hepatic veins

Feedback:
Obstruction of hepatic veins by thrombus or tumor is referred to as Budd Chiari syndrome. It can lead to symptoms and complications such as hepatic congestion, hepatomegaly, ascites, RUQ pain and jaundice. The image below demonstrates thrombus within the hepatic veins and no flow is detected by color Doppler.

column1:
If the external iliac artery is occluded, what vessels provide the potential for collateral flow to the extremity?

A: internal thoracic artery and lumbosacral artery
B: genicular arteries
C: medial and lateral plantar arteries
D: inferior epigastric artery and deep circumflex artery
column2:
Answer: D. inferior epigastric artery and deep circumflex artery

Feedback:
Lateral branches of the EIA include the inferior epigastric artery and deep circumflex artery that supply the abdominal wall muscles/skin. They also provide potential for collateralization with obstruction of the EIA

column1:
Letter K indicates which of the following vessels?

A: right portal vein
B: left hepatic vein
C: middle hepatic vein
D: left portal vein
column2:
Answer: B. left hepatic vein

Feedback:
I. right hepatic vein J. middle hepatic vein K. left hepatic vein

column1:
A patient presents with left leg claudication. The right ABI is 1.32 and the left ABI is 1.37. What should you do next for this patient?

A: switch to 16cm thigh cuffs and continue the segmental exam to include the proximal calf and thigh pressures
B: use the rapid cuff inflator to repeat the ankle pressure measurements
C: stop the segmental pressure exam and obtain toe pressures along with PVR waveforms
D: end the exam and record the findings
column2:
Answer: C. stop the segmental pressure exam and obtain toe pressures along with PVR waveforms

Feedback:
When the ABI exceeds 1.3, there is most likely medial calcification present. Because medial calcification does not usually affect the smaller digital arteries, the toe pressures can be obtained. PVR waveforms can be obtained to locate the obstruction, if the TBI is abnormal.

column1:
At the start of an endovenous ablation procedure for the great saphenous vein, the catheter is inserted _____________________ and advanced to a position_____________________ before activating the device.

A: into the distal GSV, 2cm distal to the saphenofemoral junction
B: into the GSV at the groin, 2cm below the knee
C: into the GSV at the groin, at the most distal point of reflux
D: into the distal GSV, 2cm distal to the saphenopopliteal junction
column2:
Answer: A. into the distal GSV, 2cm distal to the saphenofemoral junction

Feedback:
At the start of an endovenous ablation procedure for the GSV, the catheter is inserted into the distal vein and advanced to a position 2cm distal to the SFJ before the device is activated. Once activated, the catheter is slowly withdrawn down the leg and out through the puncture site. This should cause fibrosis of the vein in the treated area. The catheter tip must be 2cm or more from the SFJ to prevent damage to the common femoral vein.

column1:
The external carotid artery normally demonstrates a velocity below:

A: 100cm/s
B: 80cm/s
C: 150cm/s
D: 230cm/s
column2:
Answer: C. 150cm/s

Feedback:
The external carotid artery normally demonstrates a velocity below 150 cm/s

column1:
Which of the following describes a cardiovascular application of the Bernoulli Principle?

A: explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb
B: explains the relationship between pressure, resistance and flow volume
C: explains the relationship of vessel radius and flow volume
D: explains the reason for aliasing of the Doppler signal at high velocities
column2:
Answer: A. explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb

Feedback:
Bernoulli Principle explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb. When there is high velocity flow, there is low pressure and vice versa. The Bernoulli equation describes the relationship between area, velocity, and pressure at a stenosis or widening of a vessel. Poiseuille's Law explains the changes in resistance and pressure that are related to flow volume. It also describes the effects of vessel radius and length on flow volume

column1:
Where is the marginal artery of Drummond located?

A: at the splenic hilum
B: part of the cerebrovascular circulatory system
C: connects the SMA and IMA through the mesentery
D: connect the dorsalis pedis artery to the posterior tibial artery in the foot
column2:
Answer: C. connects the SMA and IMA through the mesentery

Feedback:
The marginal artery of Drummond connects the SMA and IMA through the mesentery and is a possible pathway for collateral flow.

column1:
When a patient has multiple indications for an ultrasound exam, which one is used to determine appropriate use of vascular ultrasound testing?

A: the indication with the highest score should be used to determine appropriate use
B: if a patient has multiple symptoms, the Appropriate Use Criteria is invalid
C: all scores should be added together and divided by the number of indications
D: the indication with the lowest score should be used to determine appropriate use
column2:
Answer: A. the indication with the highest score should be used to determine appropriate use

Feedback:
If there is more than one indication for an exam, the indication with the highest score should be used to determine appropriate use.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes compartment syndrome?

A: most commonly occurs due to trauma
B: typically occurs below the diaphragm in the chest
C: typically occurs below the diaphragm in the abdomen
D: caused by compression of nerves during normal extremity motion
column2:
Answer: A. most commonly occurs due to trauma

Feedback:
Acute compartment syndrome is an emergency, but chronic compartment syndrome is not an emergency. The acute onset of compartment syndrome affects the extremities and is usually associated with trauma(broken leg or arm). Increased pressure in the osteofascial compartment will prevent blood flow in and out of surrounding tissues. This can cause permanent muscle and nerve damage.

column1:
A lower extremity venous Doppler that is performed for suspected DVT should be performed with the patient in the ____________________ position.

A: reverse Trendelenburg
B: prone
C: standing
D: Trendelenburg
column2:
Answer: A. reverse Trendelenburg

Feedback:
A venous Doppler that is performed for suspected DVT should be performed with the patient in the reverse Trendelenburg position. This semi-erect position increases intra-abdominal pressure and reduces outflow to engorge the veins with blood for easier visualization.

column1:
Upon cessation of exercise, the ankle pressures in a normal patient will:

A: increase to normal levels in less than 10 minutes
B: increase to normal levels in less than 5 minutes
C: decrease to normal levels in less than 5 minutes
D: decrease to normal levels in less than 10 minutes
column2:
Answer: C. decrease to normal levels in less than 5 minutes

Feedback:
Upon cessation of exercise, the ankle pressures in a normal patient will decrease to normal levels within 5 minutes. Ankle and arm pressures should show a mild increase with exercise. Because they increase at relatively the same levels, the ABI usually stays the same or increases slightly.

column1:
The most common color change demonstrated in a lower extremity with an acute arterial occlusion is:

A: rubor
B: brawny
C: pallor
D: cyanosis
column2:
Answer: C. pallor

Feedback:
Six "P"s of Acute Arterial Occlusion: 1. Pain 2. Pallor 3. Pulselessness 4. Paresthesia 5. Paralysis 6. Polar Sensation (Cold) ***Caused by embolism, thrombus formation or trauma

column1:
Which of the following signs/symptoms is an expected finding with a carotid sinus massage?

A: dizziness
B: urticaria
C: tachycardia
D: hypertension
column2:
Answer: A. dizziness

Feedback:
The carotid sinus massage is used to evaluate a patient for hypersensitivity of the carotid sinus as the potential cause for the non-specific symptom of syncope. If there is no stenosis identified in the extracranial arteries, this procedure may be performed. The patient is placed on a tilt table and an EKG with blood pressure monitor is used to assess the patient during the procedure. A physician will massage the carotid artery at the level of the cricoid cartilage. An abnormal response will lead to hypotension (>50mmHg drop), bradycardia and feelings of dizziness or syncope. Urticaria refers to the formation of hives which is usually related to an allergic reaction.

column1:
Flow velocity in a lower extremity arterial bypass graft is considered abnormal once it falls below __________.

A: 40cm/s
B: 60cm/s
C: 30cm/s
D: 50cm/s
column2:
Answer: A. 40cm/s

Feedback:
If the flow velocity in a lower extremity arterial bypass graft falls below 40cm/s, graft failure is suspected.

column1:
What information from an angiography report can be correlated with the ultrasound results?

A: area stenosis
B: areas of turbulent flow
C: flow velocity
D: diameter stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. diameter stenosis

Feedback:
Angiography provides information on diameter stenosis of an artery because it produces a 2-dimensional longitudinal image of vessels. Area stenosis cannot be evaluated without a transverse cut of the vessels. It is limited in that it can only estimate diameter stenosis (no area stenosis), does not offer information on flow velocity, and cannot demonstrate areas of turbulent flow

column1:
Which of the following would be contraindicated in this patient?

A: thermal ablation
B: daily exercise
C: Coumadin therapy
D: compression stockings
column2:
Answer: C. Coumadin therapy

Feedback:
Venous reflux is usually treated with compression stockings, thermal or laser ablation and lifestyle changes that increase the venous flow to the heart. (exercise, elevating legs when sitting, avoid high heel shoes) Coumadin therapy would thin the blood causing further dilatation of the damaged veins.

column1:
When a clot is present on a venogram, how does it appear on the image?

A: complex mass obstructing contrast flow
B: dark solid area within the vein
C: similar to a calcification, very echogenic
D: "empty" vein with no contrast opacification
column2:
Answer: D. "empty" vein with no contrast opacification

Feedback:
Iodinated contrast is injected into the venous system at the foot. The contrast will fill any vein that is patent. Thrombus will prevent the vein from filling with contrast.

column1:
What adjustments can be made to improve the attached image of the Doppler tracing to make it of diagnostic quality?

A: Decrease velocity scale
B: Lower the baseline
C: Increase the sample volume size
D: Increase the sweep speed
column2:
Answer: B. Lower the baseline

Feedback:
Increase scale, Lower freq. probe, Change angle°, Adjust approach to decrease distance to vessel, Switch to CW.

column1: The image displayed is from the right groin in a patient with suspected venous insufficiency. Use your mouse to position the cursor over the superficial epigastric vein and click to select it.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The superficial epigastric vein is a tributary of the great saphenous vein. It drains the skin of the lower abdomen and can cause superficial varicosities in the lower abdomen if the valves are incompetent.

column1:
During a color evaluation of the common femoral vein, the patient is asked to perform the Valsalva maneuver. The cephalic flow indicated by the blue color in the CFV immediately disappears until the patient releases their muscles. The blue color returns and completely fills the vessel. What do these color Doppler findings indicate?

A: The response described is a normal result for the CFV.
B: Superficial venous insufficiency is present.
C: There is most likely an occlusive DVT in the superficial femoral vein causing the venous turbulence proximally.
D: A significant level of insufficiency is present and should be further evaluated with PW or CW Doppler techniques.
column2:
Answer: A. The response described is a normal result for the CFV.

Feedback:
When the Valsalva maneuver is applied, flow within the lower extremities should decrease significantly and/or stop completely. The loss of color flow during the Valsalva maneuver is a normal response of cessation of flow.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the Doppler evaluation of the liver vasculature displayed?

A: The hepatic artery and portal vein are demonstrated with normal flow directions.
B: The hepatic artery is hepatopetal and the portal vein is hepatofugal.
C: The hepatic artery is hepatofugal and the portal vein is hepatopetal.
D: The portal vein flow is normal, but the hepatic artery demonstrates increased resistance
column2:
Answer: B. The hepatic artery is hepatopetal and the portal vein is hepatofugal.

Feedback:
Hepatopetal = flow toward the liver; Hepatofugal = flow away from the liver; The hepatic artery and portal vein are both normally hepatopetal. The image displays ABNORMAL flow direction in the portal vein caused by portal HTN.

column1:
The supraorbital artery originates from the ophthalmic artery and joins what branch of the ECA?

A: posterior auricular artery
B: superficial temporal artery
C: vertebral artery
D: lingual artery
column2:
Answer: B. superficial temporal artery

Feedback:
The supraorbital artery originates from the ophthalmic artery and joins the superficial temporal artery(branch of the ECA). This arrangement allows for collateral flow to occur on the same side of the body if the ICA is obstructed.

column1:
A patient has a prior history of popliteal DVT and currently complains of the increasing appearance of small varicosities on the calf. Which statement below best describes his condition?

A: There is most likely a clot in the gastrocnemius veins of the calf.
B: There is most likely a Baker's cyst in the popliteal fossa causing extrinsic compression.
C: The patient is suffering from secondary varicose vein formation.
D: The patient most likely has a recurrent acute DVT.
column2:
Answer: C. The patient is suffering from secondary varicose vein formation.

Feedback:
Primary varicosities form due to abnormal valves in the superficial venous system. Secondary varicosities form if there is an associated problem in the deep system, such as previous DVT.

column1:
What is the best quantitative parameter to use to estimate internal carotid artery stenosis in patients with severe aortic valve stenosis?

A: end diastolic velocity
B: acceleration time
C: peak systolic and end diastolic velocities
D: velocity ratio
column2:
Answer: D. velocity ratio

Feedback:
Flow in the carotid arteries will be diminished in patients with significant aortic valve stenosis. When cardiac output is reduced, the flow velocities in the carotid systems will be reduced. This can lead to underestimation of ICA stenosis. The velocity ratio is used to provide a more accurate of estimate stenosis in these patients.

column1:
The most cephalic branch of the abdominal aorta is:

A: Common hepatic artery
B: Splenic artery
C: celiac axis
D: SMA
column2:
Answer: C. celiac axis

Feedback:
The celiac axis is the first abdominal branch of the aorta followed by the origin of the SMA approximately 2cm more distal on the aorta. The renal arteries are the next distal branch followed by the IMA.

column1:
A patient presents with groin pain 3 days after undergoing right femoral artery angioplasty. The image displayed is from the right groin in the area of pain. What should you report on your technologist worksheet?

A: A pseudoaneurysm is present that requires immediate intervention
B: A normal lymph node is present.
C: An abnormally enlarged lymph node is present.
D: A pseudoaneurysm is present that is nearly thrombosed
column2:
Answer: C. An abnormally enlarged lymph node is present.

Feedback:
Abnormal lymph nodes have a more rounded shape with hypoechoic/anechoic cortical tissue. The hilum will become less visible. Posterior enhancement can also be noted due to the edema in the cortex.

column1:
Before performing a liver transplant evaluation, the Sonographer must review the surgical history to determine:

A: the location of all sites of vascular anastomosis
B: if the transplant was full or partial
C: if the transplant has a piggyback or interposition anastomosis
D: more than one of the above
column2:
Answer: D. more than one of the above

Feedback:
Before beginning the exam, the surgical notes should be reviewed to determine if the patient has a full or partial transplant, a piggyback or interposition anastomosis and note the anastomosis sites for all vasculature.

column1:
The transducer is placed in the longitudinal position slightly anterior to midline of the medial upper left calf. The beam is angled posterior until the image displayed appears. What vessel is indicated by letter C?

A: anterior tibial vein
B: gastrocnemius vein
C: posterior tibial vein
D: peroneal vein
column2:
Answer: D. peroneal vein

Feedback:
The image displays the confluence of the posterior tibial and peroneal veins. The peroneal veins are deeper on the image.

column1:
Why would bilateral brachial blood pressure (BP) assessment assist in the diagnosis of a subclavian steal?

A: The highest brachial pressure is needed to calculate the ABI.
B: The systolic BP will be lower on the affected side
C: The systolic BP will be higher in the affected side
D: To evaluate the potential for future limb amputation.
column2:
Answer: B. The systolic BP will be lower on the affected side

Feedback:
For Example: If the left subclavian artery is occluded proximal to the origin of the left vertebral artery, flow from the right vertebral artery will fill the left vertebral artery in a retrograde fashion. This blood will then fill the distal subclavian artery and enter the arm. The reduced flow into the left arm will demonstrate reduced pressure.

column1:
Which of the following requires a 2D and Doppler evaluation for a complete exam?

A: Popliteal entrapment
B: Thoracic outlet syndrome
C: Raynaud syndrome
D: Renal or liver transplant
column2:
Answer: D. Renal or liver transplant

Feedback:
A renal transplant requires a 2D evaluation to assess renal size and cortical echogenicity related to the renal sinus. Doppler is used to evaluate arterial and venous flow. A liver transplant requires a 2D evaluation to assess liver echogenicity and echotexture. The other abnormalities listed would easily be evaluated with PPG or digit pressures and imaging would not necessarily be required.

column1:
The _______________ artery terminates at the external calcaneal artery.

A: peroneal
B: posterior tibial
C: anterior tibial
D: medial gastrocnemius
column2:
Answer: A. peroneal

Feedback:
The peroneal artery terminates at the external calcaneal artery.

column1:
Which of the following is not an expected finding related to the abnormality displayed on the image?

A: spontaneous flow in the distal great saphenous vein
B: varicose veins in the calf
C: dilated paratibial perforators
D: dilated posterior arch vein
column2:
Answer: A. spontaneous flow in the distal great saphenous vein

Feedback:
Severe GSV reflux would most likely lead to the formation of varicosities distally. The paratibial perforators connect the GSV to the PTVs. The GSV pressures will be increased and this will dilate the perforators. The posterior accessory saphenous vein (posterior arch vein) empties into the GSV below the knee. If the GSV is dilated, the accessory vein will also dilate. Flow in the superficial veins is not usually spontaneous. As the pressure in the GSV increases, there will be no flow toward the heart without augmentation.

column1:
The posterior tibial artery courses along:

A: the medial aspect of the thigh.
B: the medial aspect of the calf.
C: the lateral aspect of the calf.
D: the posterior aspect of the knee
column2:
Answer: B. the medial aspect of the calf.

Feedback:
The posterior tibial artery courses along the medial aspect of the calf.

column1:
A patient presents for a TCD exam with a recent diagnosis of subdural hematoma. What is the physician looking for on the TCD exam?

A: associated distal carotid stenosis
B: hematoma formation within the cranium
C: vasospasm
D: associated intracranial stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. vasospasm

Feedback:
TCD does not provide an image and US is not effective for accurate diagnosis of hematoma formation in the cranium. Intracranial stenosis would most commonly lead to ischemia not hemorrhage TCD for Vasospasm is used in patients with subarachnoid hemorrhage, usually caused by a slow bleed. Vasospasm usually occurs within 14 days of the start of the hemorrhage. Flow velocities increase in MCA, ACA and PCA with subarachnoid hemorrhage. Serial evaluations are performed to evaluate the PSV in the MCA. If hemorrhage is present, MCA peak velocity over 80cm/s indicates flow changes due to hemorrhage; MCA peak velocity over 200cm/s indicates severe significant reduction in intracerebral flow. Hemispheric Index - divide MCA velocity by the distal/terminal ICA velocity; HI > 3 ABNL

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the venous system of the upper extremity?

A: The superficial venous system of the arm is the primary drainage system of the extremity
B: The veins of the upper extremity have nearly twice the number of valves compared to the lower extremity
C: Patients with May Thurner syndrome have an increased risk of developing upper extremity thrombosis
D: Effort thrombosis refers to clot in the veins of the hand caused by repetitive wrist motion
column2:
Answer: A. The superficial venous system of the arm is the primary drainage system of the extremity

Feedback:
The superficial venous system of the arm is the primary drainage system of the extremity. The deep system is the primary drainage system in the legs. The veins of the upper extremity have far fewer valves than the lower extremity. They do not need as many because the arm is closer to the heart and experiences lower hydrostatic pressures. May Thurner syndrome affects the left common iliac vein. Effort thrombosis refers to thrombus formation at the shoulder caused by repetitive shoulder movement that compresses the vein in the thoracic outlet.

column1:
Which of the following is considered a limitation of the three cuff lower extremity segmental pressure exam?

A: There is no way to differentiate lower thigh and popliteal arterial disease.
B: The single thigh cuff normally overestimates the thigh pressure.
C: There is no way to differentiate groin and ankle arterial disease.
D: There is no way to differentiate proximal and distal calf artery disease.
column2:
Answer: A. There is no way to differentiate lower thigh and popliteal arterial disease.

Feedback:
Because there is only one cuff on the thigh, there is no way to differentiate disease in the lower thigh from disease in the popliteal artery. The pressure is obtained mid-thigh and calf. The distal SFA and popliteal artery could have disease if the calf pressure is decreased.

column1:
A thrombus is identified in the soleal veins of the calf. The thrombus will propagate into what calf veins first?

A: posterior tibial and peroneal
B: peroneal, posterior tibial and anterior tibial
C: anterior tibial
D: great saphenous
column2:
Answer: A. posterior tibial and peroneal

Feedback:
When answering questions regarding clot propagation, consider what vessel is proximal to the clot. When answering questions regarding distal flow changes due to a proximal clot, consider what vessel is emptying blood into the vessel with the clot. The soleal vessels drain into the peroneal and posterior tibial veins of the calf. A clot in the soleal veins will first extend into the posterior tibial and peroneal veins.

column1:
The Doppler tracing of the flow in a vessel just distal to a stenosis will demonstrate _____________ than at the stenosis.

A: more laminar flow
B: greater resistance
C: greater bandwidth
D: greater Doppler shift
column2:
Answer: C. greater bandwidth

Feedback:
Turbulence will cause a large range of different velocities (frequency shifts). The increased bandwidth is displayed as spectral broadening and irregular waveform contour(post stenotic turbulence).

column1:
Why does blood move into smaller collateral vessels when a larger vessel is occluded?

A: the resistance to flow in the smaller collateral is lower than the resistance in the occluded vessel
B: because of the significant drop in venous pressure
C: to compensate for loss of blood flow proximal to the obstruction
D: the resistance to flow in the smaller collateral is higher than the resistance in the occluded vessel
column2:
Answer: A. the resistance to flow in the smaller collateral is lower than the resistance in the occluded vessel

Feedback:
Collateral flow around an occlusion develops to compensate for the reduction in flow to the distal tissues. When the native artery is patent, the resistance to flow is greater in the smaller arteries. When the larger artery is occluded, the resistance to flow in the smaller collateral is lower than the resistance in the occluded vessel. Blood flow moves into the vessel with the least resistance.

column1:
Which of the following lower extremity vessels would most likely be occluded in a patient with Phlegmasia Alba Dolens?

A: saphenous veins only
B: femoral vein, deep calf veins, popliteal vein
C: deep veins, superficial veins and common femoral artery
D: common femoral artery
column2:
Answer: B. femoral vein, deep calf veins, popliteal vein

Feedback:
Phlegmasia Alba Dolens refers to a condition where all deep venous vessels are occluded in the extremity causing reduced arterial inflow. The superficial veins are usually patent. The leg has a milky white appearance due to severe outflow obstruction and markedly reducing arterial inflow. Leads to ischemia in entire extremity with severe pain.

column1:
Which of the following vessels are a common source for a Type II endoleak?

A: celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery
B: left gastric artery and right hepatic artery
C: common iliac artery and gonadal arteries
D: inferior mesenteric artery and lumbar arteries
column2:
Answer: D. inferior mesenteric artery and lumbar arteries

Feedback:
Type II endoleak: leak at the branch vessels; will see retrograde flow into the aneurysm sac and increase in AAA diameter; inferior mesenteric artery, internal iliac, lumbar arteries and accessory renal arteries are potential sources for the leak

column1:
The innominate vein is formed by the junction of:

A: internal jugular vein and superior vena cava
B: subclavian vein and internal jugular vein
C: superior vena cava and inferior vena cava
D: subclavian vein and cephalic vein
column2:
Answer: B. subclavian vein and internal jugular vein

Feedback:
The innominate vein is formed by the junction of subclavian vein and internal jugular vein.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the Doppler tracing from the renal artery?

A: Normal flow pattern and velocity are demonstrated but an incorrect Doppler angle is used to assess the flow causing aliasing to occur
B: Normal flow pattern and velocity are demonstrated
C: According to RAS diagnostic criteria, Stenosis > 60% is demonstrated
D: The renal to aortic ratio is most likely below 3.5
column2:
Answer: C. According to RAS diagnostic criteria, Stenosis > 60% is demonstrated

Feedback:
Renal artery stenosis > 60% is indicated when the PSV is greater than 2.0m/s and the renal aortic ratio is over 3.5. A 60% stenosis is considered significant and treatment is required. There are no other diagnostic criteria used to stage RAS at 75% and 85% stenosis etc.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes a normal Doppler waveform of an artery from the lower extremity?

A: There will be a low resistance, monophasic waveform with a small amount of flow reversal in diastole.
B: The triphasic waveform never demonstrates a retrograde flow component during the cardiac cycle.
C: The triphasic waveform will have two components of antegrade flow during the cardiac cycle.
D: The triphasic waveform will have two components of normal retrograde flow during the cardiac cycle.
column2:
Answer: C. The triphasic waveform will have two components of antegrade flow during the cardiac cycle.

Feedback:
The resistance in the peripheral vascular beds is very high compared to the abdominal organs. Blood flows into the extremities during systole with flow reversal during diastole. The resistance in the vascular beds is greater than the resistance in the feeding vessels during mid/end diastole causing flow reversal to occur in the vessel. This produces a waveform with at least two separate phases of flow (biphasic) If the pressure gradient between the vascular bed and the feeding vessel continues to equalize, a small amount of flow may move toward the vascular bed at the very end of diastole. This produces a waveform with three separate phases of flow (triphasic), 2 antegrade and 1 retrograde component. Biphasic and triphasic flow is generally considered normal in the extremities.

column1:
What is causing the waveform displayed?

A: proximal stenosis
B: arrhythmia
C: distal stenosis
D: subclavian steal
column2:
Answer: B. arrhythmia

Feedback:
Note the difference in timing of the systolic peaks of the waveform. This indicates a cardiac arrhythmia. In this case atrial fibrillation is present. The velocity demonstrated is normal for the vertebral arteries.

column1:
What is the most common complication of an in-situ vein graft?

A: infection
B: thrombosis
C: rupture
D: AV fistula
column2:
Answer: D. AV fistula

Feedback:
An AV fistula is the most common complication of an in-situ vein graft. Because the vein is left in its normal position and attached to the arterial system at each end, some side tributaries persist as residual arteriovenous fistulae. When the vein is connected to the arterial system, flow moves through the vein and can enter the smaller tributary veins causing an AV fistula.

column1:
Which of the following is not a common type of lower extremity graft placement?

A: femoral artery to the popliteal artery
B: popliteal artery to the DPA
C: femoral artery to the PTA
D: femoral artery to the femoral artery
column2:
Answer: B. popliteal artery to the DPA

Feedback:
A Fem-Fem bypass graft indicates connection between two segments of the same femoral artery or between the right and left femoral artery. A Fem-Pop bypass graft connects the femoral artery and popliteal artery in the same leg. A Fem-Tib graft connects the femoral artery and most commonly the tibial artery. A bypass graft could not be connected to the dorsalis pedis artery because of its size and location.

column1:
Which of the following is an autosomal dominant disorder that can lead to arteriovenous malformation in the liver?

A: Klippel–Trenaunay syndrome
B: Paget Schroetter syndrome
C: May Thurner syndrome
D: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia
column2:
Answer: D. Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia

Feedback:
Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia, AKA Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome, is an autosomal dominant disorder that affects the vasculature of multiple organs. It is characterized by multiple arteriovenous malformations (AVMs). Most commonly the AVMs are superficially located in the skin, but can also occur in pulmonary, cerebral, GI tract, and liver vasculature. Contrast echocardiography can be used to diagnose a suspected pulmonary AVM. Ultrasound is most helpful in evaluating liver AVMs. The malformations can be arteriovenous or portovenous. Ultrasound evaluation can demonstrate signs of portal HTN due to increased portal flow and increased resistance in the hepatic arterial flow with abnormal arterial connections. The disorder can cause mesenteric angina from the steal phenomenon.

column1:
Which imaging plane can produce a longitudinal image of the IVC and the aorta on the same image?

A: coronal
B: mid sagittal
C: transverse
D: coronal and transverse
column2:
Answer: A. coronal

Feedback:
Placing the patient in the decubitus position and placing the probe on the lateral aspect of the abdomen will demonstrate a longitudinal view of the aorta and IVC side by side on the image. A transverse view can also demonstrate both great vessels on the same image but will not provide a longitudinal view.

column1:
When using the 4-cuff method to obtain segmental pressures in the legs, the blood pressure in the proximal thigh normally exceeds the brachial systolic pressure by __________mmHg.

A: 10-20
B: 20-30
C: 30-40
D: 40-50
column2:
Answer: C. 30-40

Feedback:
When performing the 4-cuff method of obtaining segmental pressures, the blood pressure in the proximal thigh normally exceeds the brachial systolic pressure by 30-40mmHg. The 3-cuff method yields a thigh pressure that should be equivalent to the brachial pressure in a normal pressure.

column1:
Which of the following will decrease with acute renal allograft rejection?

A: kidney size
B: resistive index from the arcuate arteries
C: diastolic flow in the segmental arteries
D: echogenicity of the renal cortex
column2:
Answer: C. diastolic flow in the segmental arteries

Feedback:
Acute renal rejection will result in an enlarged kidney with increased echogenicity of the renal cortex. Doppler evaluation will demonstrate increased resistance in the parenchymal arteries and increased resistive index. This will decrease the amount of diastolic flow in the cortical arteries.

column1:
A fem - fem graft is used to treat:

A: bilateral popliteal stenosis
B: bilateral iliac stenosis
C: unilateral femoral stenosis
D: unilateral iliac stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. unilateral iliac stenosis

Feedback:
A fem - fem graft can be used to correct unilateral iliac stenosis. Blood is passed from the patent femoral artery through the graft to the contralateral femoral artery at a point distal to the stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following are common symptoms of chronic venous disease?

A: pitting edema and ulceration on great toe
B: pallor and paresthesia
C: brawny discoloration and pitting edema
D: varicosities and pallor
column2:
Answer: C. brawny discoloration and pitting edema

Feedback:
Toe ulceration, pallor and paresthesia are signs of arterial disease. Varicosities, brawny discoloration and pitting edema are signs of chronic venous disease.

column1:
An abnormality in which of the following vessels could be the cause of vasculogenic impotence?

A: common and internal iliac arteries
B: internal and external iliac arteries
C: aorta and gonadal arteries
D: external iliac and femoral arteries
column2:
Answer: A. common and internal iliac arteries

Feedback:
The external iliac artery supplies blood to the leg. The internal iliac artery supplies blood to the branches that supply the penis. If there are flow abnormalities in the aorta, internal iliac artery or common iliac artery, this can lead to vasculogenic impotence. The gonadal arteries originate from the aorta and supply blood to the scrotum and testicles.

column1:
A patient presents for Carotid Doppler exam following a recent MVA. What is demonstrated on the images provided from this patient's exam?

A: Normal images of CCA flow
B: Pseudoaneurysm of the CCA
C: Improper color Doppler scale settings
D: Dissection
column2:
Answer: D. Dissection

Feedback:
The history of a recent MVA should lead to a possible diagnosis of either a pseudoaneurysm or dissection. The images displayed demonstrate an intimal flap with flow reversal. Note the distinct separation of layers and flow direction during the two phases of the cardiac cycle.

column1:
Which of the following is an indirect sign of an unstable AAA sac with an endoleak?

A: Echolucent areas within the sac
B: Reduction in aneurysm shape and/or sac size
C: Absence of pulsatility of the sac
D: Triphasic flow in the iliac arteries
column2:
Answer: A. Echolucent areas within the sac

Feedback:
Potential signs of an unstable AAA sac: Echolucent areas within the sac - fresh blood leaking out of graft and into sac Changes in aneurysm shape and/or sac size - increase in size and shape changes indicate blood flowing into the sac Increased pulsatility of the sac - the walls of the AAA become thinner as it stretches with fresh blood internally, this causes the sac to pulse more with cardiac contractions.

column1:
Which of the following vessels does not drain into the IVC?

A: right gonadal vein
B: right renal vein
C: left hepatic vein
D: splenic vein
column2:
Answer: D. splenic vein

Feedback:
The left gonadal vein empties into the left renal vein while the right gonadal vein empties directly into the IVC. The splenic vein is part of the portal system.

column1:
Which waveform can be described as biphasic?

A: C
B: A
C: B
D: D
column2:
Answer: C. B

Feedback:
A. monophasic, high resistance B. biphasic C. Triphasic D. monophasic, low resistance

column1:
In a patient with a patent brachiobasilic AV graft, the axillary vein will:

A: demonstrate low velocity continuous flow
B: demonstrate a loss of spontaneous and phasic flow and will require distal augmentation to assess patency
C: demonstrate biphasic flow with the majority of antegrade flow occurring during diastole
D: demonstrate high velocity pulsatile flow
column2:
Answer: D. demonstrate high velocity pulsatile flow

Feedback:
The basilic vein connects to the axillary vein in the upper arm/axilla. The arterial inflow into the basilic vein from the AVG will transmit a pulsatile waveform with high velocities into the axillary vein.

column1:
A patient presents for a venous insufficiency exam. The exam demonstrates normal bilateral saphenous veins that extend up to the distal thigh, but do not appear to connect to the deep system. What should you review in the patient chart to explain these findings?

A: medical history for Paget Schroetter syndrome
B: surgical history for venous ligation of the bilateral GSVs
C: treatment history for RF ablation of the bilateral GSVs
D: medical history for Klippel Trenaunay Webber syndrome
column2:
Answer: B. surgical history for venous ligation of the bilateral GSVs

Feedback:
Ligation of the proximal segment of the GSV could produce these findings. Prior to the introduction of endovenous treatment, ligation of the diseased segments was commonly performed to treat reflux. RF ablation would affect a larger segment of the GSV. Klippel Trenaunay Webber syndrome refers to the congenital absence of the deep venous system of the legs. Paget Schroetter syndrome refers to thrombosis of the subclavian vein due to excessive motion, usually related to TOS.

column1:
The string flow sign is associated with which of the following diagnoses?

A: fibromuscular dysplasia
B: critical ICA stenosis
C: AV fistula
D: superficial phlebitis
column2:
Answer: B. critical ICA stenosis

Feedback:
The string sign refers to a critical stenosis that only allows a thin string of blood through the tiny opening. The flow velocity will be very low with decreased pulsatility. The risk for total occlusion is extremely high in these cases. FMD causes the appearance of a string of beads.

column1:
Which of the following are common risk factors for varicose vein formation?

A: smoking and high cholesterol
B: pregnancy and standing for long periods of time
C: diabetes and smoking
D: post-phlebitic syndrome and high cholesterol
column2:
Answer: B. pregnancy and standing for long periods of time

Feedback:
Smoking, high cholesterol and diabetes are risk factors for atherosclerotic disease. Post-phlebitic syndrome, pregnancy, and standing for long periods of time.

column1:
Which of the following will maintain compliance with the ALARA principle?

A: reducing the number of images taken in standard protocol
B: reducing overall gain
C: reducing transducer frequency
D: reducing the amount of protected information given to the patient
column2:
Answer: A. reducing the number of images taken in standard protocol

Feedback:
As Low As Reasonably Achievable refers to the exposure to ultrasound. Minimizing the number of standard images taken on each patient will reduce the exam time and exposure. Reducing frequency and gain do not affect the intensity/exposure time.

column1:
Which of the following describes the path of an embolus?

A: a small piece of soft plaque breaks off from the carotid bulb and floats toward the lungs
B: a small piece of soft plaque breaks off from the carotid bulb and floats toward the brain
C: a small piece of thrombus breaks off from a thrombus in the left ventricle and floats toward the lungs
D: a small piece of thrombus breaks off from a thrombus in the right ventricle and floats toward the brain
column2:
Answer: B. a small piece of soft plaque breaks off from the carotid bulb and floats toward the brain

Feedback:
An embolus is a floating piece of debris, air bubble, thrombus or plaque that moves distally until it lodges in a small vessel. Thrombus in the right ventricle will be sent to pulmonary artery and the lungs = pulmonary embolism. Thrombus in the left ventricle can be sent to the brain through the aorta = stroke. An embolus in the carotid system will go to the brain.

column1:
Bilateral bruits are detected in the proximal neck. This finding is most suggestive of:

A: CHF
B: Polycythemia vera
C: Subclavian steal
D: Aortic valve stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. Aortic valve stenosis

Feedback:
Bilateral carotid bruits and changes in both carotid flow patterns can be related to aortic stenosis. If the aortic valve is stenotic causing increased flow velocity and turbulence, flow in both carotids will reflect this change and may demonstrate bilateral bruits. CHF would lead to decreased flow through both carotids but a bruit would most likely NOT be identified due to slow moving flow.

column1:
A patient presents with groin pain three days after percutaneous balloon angioplasty of the distal right femoral artery. The image on the left is from the common femoral artery and the image on the right is from the proximal femoral artery. Which of the following explains the findings on the exam?

A: AV fistula in the right CFA/CFV
B: The profunda femoral artery is occluded
C: There is a significant stenosis in the right iliac artery
D: Unsuccessful angioplasty procedure
column2:
Answer: A. AV fistula in the right CFA/CFV

Feedback:
The angiography procedure was performed on the distal femoral artery. The images do not demonstrate that area, so the success of the procedure cannot be evaluated. Atherosclerotic disease normally causes lower resistance flow in all vessels distal to a stenosis, not just in the vessel affected. The flow in the CFA is low resistance and monophasic but returns to high resistance and triphasic in the superficial femoral artery. This is a classic sign of the presence of an AV fistula which reduces the resistance at the site of the fistula and flow returns to normal more distally.

column1:
The soleal plexus is located:

A: In the calf
B: In the neck
C: In the forearm
D: In the Circle of Willis of the brain
column2:
Answer: A. In the calf

Feedback:
The soleal plexus is the venous drainage system/reservoir of the soleal muscle of the calf.

column1:
Which of the following is an expected finding with an AV fistula caused by an interventional procedure?

A: continuous venous outflow
B: direct connection between a single artery and vein
C: dilated venous collaterals adjacent to the fistula
D: high resistance arterial inflow
column2:
Answer: B. direct connection between a single artery and vein

Feedback:
An AVM is a congenital malformation with multiple connections between the arterioles and venules without a capillary bed in between. Venous collaterals will develop and dilate over time. An AVF is a direct connection of an artery or vein caused by trauma or interventional procedure. Due to the acute formation of the connection, there are no collaterals present. The arterial inflow and venous outflow will demonstrate the same hemodynamic characteristics in an AVM and AVF, increased arterial flow volume, low resistance arterial inflow and pulsatile, turbulent venous outflow.

column1:
Which of the following could cause a false reduction in ankle pressure on an average sized patient?

A: medial calcification of the calf vessels
B: cuff is loosely wrapped around the arm
C: using the 12cm cuff for the ankle
D: evaluating the pressure with the patient seated in a chair
column2:
Answer: C. using the 12cm cuff for the ankle

Feedback:
False elevation of the ankle pressure = medial calcification, cuff too loose, cuff too small, patient not supine (hydrostatic pressure) False reduction in the ankle pressure = cuff too large, congestive heart failure, coarctation

column1:
The Achilles tendon is a landmark for which deep calf vein?

A: peroneal vein
B: anterior tibial vein
C: posterior tibial vein
D: small saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: C. posterior tibial vein

Feedback:
The posterior tibial veins are identified at the area of the medial malleolus. The posterior tibial veins drain blood from the plantar arches and then course cephalad laying anterior to the tendon and posterior to the medial malleolus/tibial bone. The SSV is located posterior to the tendon, but it is a superficial vein.

column1:
A patient presents with HTN, DM and left lower extremity pain that worsens as he walks. He usually begins to have pain after he walks to his mailbox with increasing pain as he returns to the house. The pain is relieved by resting his leg for a few minutes. A bilateral resting ABI is performed with the right leg, 1.12 and the left leg 1.34. Which of the following statements is true regarding the findings?

A: Blood pressure and PPG evaluation of the digital arteries should be performed.
B: These symptoms are most likely unrelated to arterial disease.
C: The patient should be evaluated for popliteal entrapment in the left leg.
D: Hypertensive disease usually leads to overestimation of the ABI values.
column2:
Answer: A. Blood pressure and PPG evaluation of the digital arteries should be performed.

Feedback:
Patients with diabetes have increased incidence of medial calcification of the extremity vessels which causes falsely increased ABI measurements. An ABI over 1.30 usually indicates the presence of medial calcification. It is necessary to evaluate the digital pressures for an accurate pressure index (Toe-Brachial Index) and PPG evaluation would also aid in flow characterization.

column1:
Hydrostatic venous pressure ___________ when a patient goes from standing to the supine position.

A: increases
B: will show little change
C: shows a quick decrease before returning to 100mmHg
D: decreases
column2:
Answer: D. decreases

Feedback:
Hydrostatic pressure refers to the weight of the column of blood within the veins when a patient is standing. The pressure is 0mmHg at the level of the heart and increases more distally to a level of 100mmHg at the ankles. When a patient lies down, the pressure in the "column" is dispersed.

column1:
When evaluating a patient for placement of an AV fistula for dialysis, what is the minimum acceptable diameter for the native vein where the graft will be connected?

A: 3.5mm
B: 2.5mm
C: 4.5mm
D: 5mm
column2:
Answer: B. 2.5mm

Feedback:
Hemodialysis requires a native vein >2.5mm for AVF; native artery >2mm. A native vein >4mm is required for a synthetic graft.

column1:
A patient is undergoing AAA repair in the distal aorta. The physician places an endovascular graft within the aorta. If the graft placement was successful, how should the ankle pressures change post-procedure?

A: varies with type of graft used
B: mild decrease
C: mild increase
D: no change should be seen
column2:
Answer: C. mild increase

Feedback:
The AAA would have caused a reduction in flow moving to the legs. The lower extremities would exhibit decreased resistance in the vascular beds to maintain flow. This would indicate a mild reduction in ankle pressure. When the graft is placed in the aorta, flow to the legs increases and pressure and resistance would both increase at the ankles.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes cuff placement for a segmental pressure exam?

A: Once the patient is supine, ask him/her to raise their foot about 6 inches above the table to you can properly wrap the cuff around the leg
B: Cuffs should be placed straight around the limb, rather than angled to the curve of the limb
C: The proximal edge of the cuff should always cover a bony structure like the malleolus or tibial tubercle
D: The width of the cuff should be at least 20% narrower than the girth of the limb segment
column2:
Answer: B. Cuffs should be placed straight around the limb, rather than angled to the curve of the limb

Feedback:
The bladder of the cuff must be placed over the artery being evaluated and only compress soft tissue, not bony structures. The below-knee or upper calf cuff should be placed just distal to the tibial tubercle or pressure readings will be falsely elevated. The lower calf cuff should be placed with the distal edge 2-3cm above the medial malleolus. The width of the cuff should be at least 20% wider than the girth of the limb segment. The patient should rest the leg during cuff placement. Raising the leg causes muscle contraction and when released, the cuff will be too loose. Cuffs should be placed straight around the limb, rather than angled to the curve of the limb.

column1:
Adventitial cysts:

A: are usually treated with bypass graft placement
B: are more common in women
C: most commonly affect the popliteal artery
D: are a normal variant that can be seen in up to 10% of patients
column2:
Answer: C. most commonly affect the popliteal artery

Feedback:
Adventitial cysts are a rare condition that involves the collection of mucinous material within the adventitial wall layer of the affected vessel. They predominantly affect the peripheral arteries with the popliteal artery most commonly affected. The cysts are most common in young to middle-aged men. They can be treated by ultrasound guided cyst aspiration.

column1:
An ABI of 0.77 indicates ________________ disease.

A: severe
B: minimal
C: unable to determine without exercise portion
D: mild - moderate
column2:
Answer: D. mild - moderate

Feedback:
ABI Criteria Minimal disease 0.9 - 1.0 Mild/Moderate disease 0.5 - 0.9 Severe disease <0.5

column1:
The type of plaque that carries the greatest risk of embolization is ______________.

A: soft
B: complex
C: calcific
D: soft and complex
column2:
Answer: A. soft

Feedback:
Soft plaque has the greatest risk of embolization due to its composition and decreased adhesive levels. Complex and calcific plaque have become increasingly fibrous and more easily adheres to the vessel wall.

column1:
What arteries are most commonly affected by Takayasu arteritis?

A: subclavian and common carotid
B: calf
C: forearm
D: subclavian and brachial
column2:
Answer: A. subclavian and common carotid

Feedback:
Takayasu Arteritis causes hypertrophic area(s) of inflamed tissue usually found in the aorta and its branches. It is associated with supravalvular stenosis and aortic coarctation. 90% of cases involve the subclavian arteries and over half of the cases involve the common carotid arteries. It can also affect the renal arteries. Most commonly seen in young females (20-40yrs) and more common in Asia and India than the US. Symptoms include absence of peripheral pulse, asymmetric brachial blood pressures, amaurosis fugax, hemiparesis, diplopia, vertigo and upper extremity claudication.

column1:
Which of the following lists the most common anatomic variation in aortic arch anatomy?

A: Two branches originate from the arch: a shared innominate/left CCA trunk and the left subclavian artery
B: Pulmonary sling
C: The coronary arteries originate from the innominate artery or the right CCA
D: Double aortic arch
column2:
Answer: A. Two branches originate from the arch: a shared innominate/left CCA trunk and the left subclavian artery

Feedback:
The most common anatomic variant of the arch branches is only two direct branches of the arch: 1. The innominate and left CCA originate as one short segment and then split 2. Left subclavian artery

column1:
The common femoral artery velocity is 0.8 m/s and the proximal femoral artery demonstrates atherosclerosis formation causing an increase in the velocity to 3.2 m/s. This indicates what level of stenosis?

A: 25%
B: 50%
C: 75%
D: 100%
column2:
Answer: C. 75%

Feedback:
When the velocity between two extremity arterial segments doubles, this indicates at least a 50% stenosis. When the velocity between two extremity arterial segments quadruples, this indicates at least a 75% stenosis.

column1:
Paired veins that accompany an artery of the same name are called:

A: Klippel Trenauney Weber syndrome
B: posterior arch veins
C: duplicated veins
D: venae comitantes
column2:
Answer: D. venae comitantes

Feedback:
Concomitant veins or venae comitantes refers to a vein that is usually paired, with a vein lying on either side of a single artery of the same name. Radial, ulnar, PTV, ATV, peroneal and gastrocnemius veins are example of concomitant veins

column1:
Which of the following explains the increased incidence of DVT in the left leg when compared to the right leg?

A: The left iliac vein is usually smaller than the right.
B: Left iliac vein is a common location for DVT formation with pregnancy
C: The anatomic course of the left iliac vein leads to increased risk of extrinsic compression.
D: There are more valves present in the left iliac vein which can lead to increased venous stasis.
column2:
Answer: C. The anatomic course of the left iliac vein leads to increased risk of extrinsic compression.

Feedback:
The left iliac vein courses beneath the right iliac artery at the aortic bifurcation. Extrinsic compression has been linked to increased incidence of left leg DVT. This condition is called May Thurner syndrome.

column1:
The image demonstrated is from a routine carotid ultrasound. Based on this image, what should be done next to evaluate this patient?

A: Evaluate the right subclavian artery for the presence of monophasic flow
B: Perform a TCD exam to look for a contralateral MCA for obstruction
C: Evaluate the left subclavian artery for the presence of monophasic flow
D: Perform a TCD exam to look for a basilar obstruction
column2:
Answer: D. Perform a TCD exam to look for a basilar obstruction

Feedback:
When a normally low resistance waveform is now triphasic, it indicates a distal obstruction near to the sampling site. The high resistance triphasic waveform in a cerebrovascular artery indicates a distal obstruction. This waveform in the vertebral artery indicates a distal obstruction, which could be the basilar artery. The vertebral arteries merge to form the basilar artery. A TCD exam could identify an obstruction in the basilar artery that would explain this vertebral waveform. If the other vertebral artery is also high resistance, this would also support a basilar obstruction.

column1:
Why is an acute arterial occlusion in the lower extremity considered a critical finding?

A: the risk of arterial rupture is significantly increased
B: collaterals have not had time to form to redistribute the blood
C: the risk of stroke is significantly increased
D: because it is not treatable
column2:
Answer: B. collaterals have not had time to form to redistribute the blood

Feedback:
Without collaterals present to redistribute blood flow, the risk of tissue death and possible amputation is increased

column1:
Which of the following vessels supply the majority of blood to the cerebellum?

A: ECA and Basilar
B: Vertebral and Basilar
C: ICA and Vertebral
D: ICA and ECA
column2:
Answer: B. Vertebral and Basilar

Feedback:
The cerebellum is located in the posterior fossa of the skull. The vertebral arteries course between the vertebral pedicles to converge into the basilar artery. Branches from the vertebral and basilar arteries feed the cerebellum and portions of the brain stem.

column1:
Which of the following is a sonographic finding of acute DVT?

A: Engorged vein with increased echogenicity in the lumen
B: Flattened, collapsed vessel with increased echogenicity of the wall
C: Valvular insufficiency
D: Partial compressibility of the vein
column2:
Answer: A. Engorged vein with increased echogenicity in the lumen

Feedback:
Chronic thrombus will demonstrate as echogenic material lining a partially collapsed vein. Acute thrombus causes the vein to grow in size and appear engorged with blood/debris. NORMAL IJV - Flattened, flexible shape THROMBOSED IJV - Engorged, non-compressible

column1:
You identify flow moving toward the transducer in the left ophthalmic artery and flow moving away from the transducer in the right ophthalmic artery. Which of the following explains these findings?

A: critical stenosis in the left ACA
B: critical stenosis in the distal right ICA
C: critical stenosis in the distal left ICA
D: critical stenosis in the right ACA
column2:
Answer: B. critical stenosis in the distal right ICA

Feedback:
Flow in the ophthalmic arteries is normally evaluated in the orbital window and demonstrates flow toward the transducer. If the flow is reversed in one of the vessels, this indicates a critical stenosis in the ipsilateral ICA with ECA to ICA collateral flow.

column1:
Venography is usually performed with the patient in what position?

A: Supine with table at 60-degree reverse Trendelenburg
B: Lithotomy position
C: Supine with table at 60-degree Trendelenburg
D: Supine with table horizontal
column2:
Answer: A. Supine with table at 60-degree reverse Trendelenburg

Feedback:
Venography is performed with the patient in the semi-erect position to dilate the veins, making them more visible. When evaluating a patient for insufficiency, the semi-erect position helps to demonstrate the reflux that occurs when the patient is standing. When the patient lays on the table, the table can be adjusted to a 60-degree reverse Trendelenburg position to dilate the veins and increase hydrostatic pressure.

column1:
In most patients with a central venous catheter the tip of the catheter is positioned in the:

A: proximal subclavian vein
B: internal jugular vein
C: distal 1/3 of the superior vena cava
D: distal subclavian vein
column2:
Answer: C. distal 1/3 of the superior vena cava

Feedback:
The terminal tip of the venous catheter is positioned in a central vein, most commonly the distal 1/3 of the SVC (atriocaval junction). High blood flow volume in this location allows infusates to be rapidly distributed.

column1:
Which modality is preferred to diagnose fibromuscular dysplasia in the circle of Willis?

A: CTA
B: Angiography
C: Transcranial Doppler
D: MRI
column2:
Answer: B. Angiography

Feedback:
Cerebral angiography provides the best detail for the lumen characteristics of the intracerebral arteries.

column1:
Where is the most common site of stenosis in a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt?

A: hepatic artery anastomosis
B: hepatic vein anastomosis
C: portal vein anastomosis
D: biliary anastomosis
column2:
Answer: B. hepatic vein anastomosis

Feedback:
The hepatic vein anastomosis is the most common site of stenosis in a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt.

column1:
What structure compresses the artery in popliteal entrapment?

A: medial head of the gastrocnemius muscle
B: medial meniscus
C: lateral meniscus
D: patella
column2:
Answer: A. medial head of the gastrocnemius muscle

Feedback:
Popliteal entrapment is the compression of the artery by the medial head of the gastrocnemius muscle. It is commonly found in runners and athletes and is the most common cause of unilateral claudication in young patients.

column1:
A normal lower extremity arterial response to reactive hyperemia is:

A: no change in ankle pressures
B: <35% decrease in ankle pressures that return to baseline within one minute
C: <50% increase in ankle pressures with return to baseline in under 5 minutes
D: <50% decrease in ankle pressures that return to baseline within one minute
column2:
Answer: B. <35% decrease in ankle pressures that return to baseline within one minute

Feedback:
A normal lower extremity arterial response to reactive hyperemia is <35% drop in ankle pressures that return to normal within 1 minute. By cutting off arterial inflow for a few minutes, the extremity becomes "starved" for oxygenated blood. When the cuff is released, the inflow should return to baseline levels quickly if there is no disease present to stop the inflow.

column1:
If a patient forgets to take their hypertension medication before a renal duplex exam, what effect can this have on the findings?

A: underestimation of peak renal artery velocity
B: underestimation of renal artery stenosis
C: overestimation of renal parenchymal thickness
D: overestimation of renal artery stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. overestimation of renal artery stenosis

Feedback:
Elevated systemic BP can cause increased velocity in the systemic arteries. If the pressure is high, velocity measurements will be overestimated and renal artery stenosis can be overestimated. The patient should be evaluated when the HTN is controlled, whenever possible.

column1:
Which vein is best evaluated for compressibility by asking the patient to perform a quick, deep sniff?

A: great saphenous vein
B: axillary vein
C: distal IVC
D: subclavian vein
column2:
Answer: D. subclavian vein

Feedback:
Manual compression of the subclavian vein is limited due to its position beneath the clavicle. If the patient performs a quick, deep sniff, the normal subclavian vein should demonstrate compression.

column1:
Which of the following terms can be used to describe the normal Doppler waveform in the proximal renal vein?

A: mild pulsatility with no respiratory changes
B: continuous flow
C: respiratory phasicity with no pulsatility
D: respiratory phasicity and mild pulsatility
column2:
Answer: D. respiratory phasicity and mild pulsatility

Feedback:
Venous flow in the IVC, hepatic and proximal renal veins normally demonstrate respiratory phasicity and mild cardiac pulsatility. At the renal hilum, the flow is more continuous with minimal respiratory phasicity. Image Credit: D'Andrea A, Del Giudice C, Fabiani D, Caputo A, Sabatella F, Cante L, Palermi S, Desiderio A, Tagliamonte E, Liccardo B, et al. The Incremental Role of Multiorgan Point-of-Care Ultrasounds in the Emergency Setting. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health . 2023; 20(3):2088. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph20032088

column1:
Which of the following can lead to a false negative diagnosis of ICA stenosis?

A: Congestive heart failure
B: Anemia
C: Increased cardiac output
D: Tortuosity
column2:
Answer: A. Congestive heart failure

Feedback:
Decreased cardiac output can lead to decreased flow velocities throughout the body. CHF can lead to a false negative diagnosis for stenosis due to abnormally decreased velocities in the carotid system. Anemia causes the blood to thin and flow velocities would increase.

column1:
Which of the following is a cause for deep venous HTN?

A: Diffuse atherosclerotic disease
B: Klippel Trenaunay Webber Syndrome
C: Congestive heart failure
D: Aortic coarctation
column2:
Answer: C. Congestive heart failure

Feedback:
Venous HTN is described as increased venous volume that causes fluid to leak into interstitial spaces between tissues. Related to thrombus formation, venous insufficiency, CHF, pregnancy

column1:
A patient presents with a script that requests an aortic Doppler evaluation due to Leriche syndrome. What are you looking for on the exam?

A: monophasic flow in all segments of the aorta due to coarctation of the aorta in the chest
B: agenesis of the anterior branches of the aorta
C: the formation of multiple aneurysms of the aortic segments
D: extensive diffuse atherosclerotic disease causing obstruction of the distal aorta
column2:
Answer: D. extensive diffuse atherosclerotic disease causing obstruction of the distal aorta

Feedback:
Leriche syndrome refers to extensive diffuse atherosclerotic disease causing obstruction of the distal aorta and bilateral lower extremity symptoms.

column1:
Why is the carotid compression technique performed?

A: to evaluate the venous outflow of the intracranial vessels
B: to evaluate the presence of intracranial collateral formation
C: to evaluate the carotid as a potential arterial graft
D: to evaluate the effects of vasospasm
column2:
Answer: B. to evaluate the presence of intracranial collateral formation

Feedback:
Carotid Compression Test is performed by a PHYSICIAN ONLY!! Used to evaluate the risk for carotid treatment; Compress unilateral CCA for approximately 5 minutes, if no symptoms occur, collaterals are present that are compensating for reduced flow.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the vertebral arteries?

A: The right vertebral artery is the first branch of the aortic arch.
B: The right vertebral artery is normally smaller than the left.
C: The right and left vertebral arteries empty their blood into the right and left basilar arteries.
D: Vertebral artery flow is normally biphasic due to turbulence at their junction with the basilar artery.
column2:
Answer: B. The right vertebral artery is normally smaller than the left.

Feedback:
The right vertebral artery is normally smaller than the left vertebral artery. It originates from the right subclavian artery. The right and left vertebral arteries merge distally to form a single basilar artery. Normal Doppler evaluation demonstrates a low resistance, monophasic waveform.

column1:
During a TCD exam the depth is set to 100mm while using the suboccipital window. What vessel will be interrogated and what direction will the flow be moving?

A: basilar, toward the transducer
B: carotid siphon, varies with portion of the siphon evaluated
C: vertebral, toward the transducer
D: basilar, away from the transducer
column2:
Answer: D. basilar, away from the transducer

Feedback:
60 - 90mm suboccipital window = vertebral 80 - 120mm suboccipital window = basilar Both vessels will normally exhibit flow moving away from the transducer and toward the brain

column1:
Arterial claudication occurs:

A: distal to the obstruction
B: while the patient is resting
C: proximal to the obstruction
D: while the legs are in the dependent position
column2:
Answer: A. distal to the obstruction

Feedback:
Arterial claudication occurs distal to the hemodynamically significant obstruction. The tissues distal to the obstruction are not receiving the necessary oxygen which causes cramping and pain.

column1:
This Doppler tracing taken from a branch of the aorta demonstrates:

A: Normal celiac flow with top normal PSV
B: Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the right renal artery
C: Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the left renal artery
D: Critical stenosis of the celiac axis
column2:
Answer: D. Critical stenosis of the celiac axis

Feedback:
Note the spectral broadening, increased PSV over 5 m/s (<2.0 NL) and increased diastolic flow. These Doppler characteristics should lead to a diagnosis of hemodynamically significant stenosis of the celiac artery. The renal arteries would not normally be evaluated with Doppler in the longitudinal view of the aorta. They also would not originate from the anterior aorta.

column1:
The most common normal variant in the external/internal carotid artery anatomy is:

A: the ECA lies posterior and medial to the ICA
B: the ECA is absent and the ICA has branches that feed the extracranial structures
C: the ECA branches directly from the ICA at the level of the angle of the mandible
D: the ECA lies posterior and lateral to the ICA
column2:
Answer: D. the ECA lies posterior and lateral to the ICA

Feedback:
In most patients the ECA is the anterior medial branch of CCA. There are several normal variants in ECA/ICA position, with the most common being the ECA lies posterior and lateral to the ICA.

column1:
The following pressure readings from a segmental pressure evaluation were obtained in a patient suffering from low back and thigh pain. What is the ABI for each leg? Lt Ankle 106 Rt Ankle 98 Lt Arm 124 Rt Arm 134

A: Lt = 0.85, Rt = 0.73
B: Lt = 0.85, Rt = 0.79
C: Lt = 0.79, Rt = 0.73
D: Lt = 1.17, Rt = 1.36
column2:
Answer: C. Lt = 0.79, Rt = 0.73

Feedback:
Lt ABI = 106/134 = 0.79 Rt = 98/134 = 0.73; always use the highest brachial pressure for both ABI calculations. You should not have complicated math problems on the registry exam. The most important part of any calculation is to know how to set up the calculation. The highest calf artery pressure is divided by the highest brachial pressure to calculate the ABI.

column1:
You are performing a mesenteric duplex examination for suspected mesenteric ischemia. The findings are: SMA PSV 408cm/s, EDV 78cm/s, monophasic flow with turbulence Celiac PSV 300cm/s, EDV 55cm/s, monophasic flow with spectral window IMA PSV 180cm/s, EDV 25cm/s, monophasic flow with spectral window How will you report these findings?

A: isolated stenosis of the celiac artery with compensatory flow in the SMA and IMA
B: isolated stenosis of the SMA with compensatory flow in the celiac artery and IMA
C: suspected mesenteric ischemia with abnormal flow velocities and waveforms in the SMA and celiac artery
D: suspected mesenteric ischemia with abnormal flow velocities and waveforms in the SMA, IMA, and celiac artery
column2:
Answer: B. isolated stenosis of the SMA with compensatory flow in the celiac artery and IMA

Feedback:
If there is significant obstruction or occlusion of one mesenteric artery and the other two main arteries are normal, there can be associated changes in these normal vessels. If the SMA is occluded, the celiac artery may demonstrate flow with increased velocity and a normal spectral window (no post-stenotic turbulence). If the SMA is occluded, the IMA can increase in size and demonstrate increased flow velocity without post-stenotic turbulence. If the celiac artery is occluded, the SMA and/or IMA can demonstrate increased flow velocity without post-stenotic turbulence. Keep in mind that compensatory flow can mimic stenosis. It is very important to evaluate the waveform characteristics with elevated systolic velocities. Compensatory flow = increased velocity + spectral window Stenosis = increased velocity + post-stenotic turbulence

column1:
During a TCD exam the depth is set to 55mm with a slight anterior angulation while using the temporal window. What vessel will be interrogated and what direction will the flow be moving?

A: middle cerebral artery, toward the transducer
B: anterior cerebral artery, away from the transducer
C: posterior cerebral artery, away from the transducer
D: middle cerebral artery, away from the transducer
column2:
Answer: A. middle cerebral artery, toward the transducer

Feedback:
MCA = 30-60mm depth at temporal window; normally demonstrates flow moving toward the transducer

column1:
Increased resistance in a renal artery can indicate:

A: normal post-prandial flow
B: normal pre-prandial flow
C: distal stenosis or kidney disease
D: proximal stenosis or kidney disease
column2:
Answer: C. distal stenosis or kidney disease

Feedback:
Increased resistance in a renal artery can indicate distal stenosis or kidney disease. If the renal artery demonstrates increased resistance to flow, there is either a vascular blockage distally or the kidney parenchyma is resisting the arterial inflow because it cannot properly filter the blood. Any kidney disease that affects the filtration system will lead to increased parenchymal resistance to arterial inflow. Normal renal artery flow is low resistance with antegrade diastolic flow.

column1:
The highest velocity in the normal common carotid artery is usually identified:

A: in the first 3cm of the vessel
B: < 3cm from the bulb
C: mid CCA
D: <1cm from the bulb
column2:
Answer: A. in the first 3cm of the vessel

Feedback:
The highest ICA velocity is usually identified in the first 3cm of the vessel. The highest CCA velocity is also usually identified in the proximal artery.

column1:
