Which of the following is true regarding the initial post-op examination of a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt placement?

A: It should be performed within 30 days following the procedure.
B: Marked ascites is a common finding for the initial post op exam on a TIPS patient.
C: It helps to establish baseline velocities for upcoming follow up exams.
D: CTA is most commonly performed for the initial evaluation of a TIPS procedure.
column2:
Answer: C. It helps to establish baseline velocities for upcoming follow up exams.

Feedback:
When performing serial evaluations of a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt, it is important to establish a baseline for the patient very soon after the shunt placement. The initial evaluation should be performed with Doppler ultrasound within 24hrs of shunt placement.

column1:
Which of the following is a normal response from the capillary beds of the feet and ankles after 5 minutes of vigorous exercise?

A: vasodilation
B: increased osmotic pressures
C: no change in the flow in normal vessels
D: vasoconstriction
column2:
Answer: A. vasodilation

Feedback:
Resistance decreases in the capillary beds to allow an increase in flow volume with exercise. Normal arteries do not demonstrate any change in pressure with exercise.

column1:
The resistive index of the renal parenchymal arteries in cases of main renal artery stenosis will _________________ and the resistive index of the renal parenchymal arteries in cases of renal parenchymal disease will _________________

A: increase, remain normal
B: increase, decrease
C: decrease, increase
D: remain normal, decrease
column2:
Answer: C. decrease, increase

Feedback:
The resistive index of the renal parenchymal arteries in cases of main renal artery stenosis will decrease to below 0.5. The resistance distal to a significant stenosis decreases and a tardus parvus waveform is possible. The resistive index of the renal parenchymal arteries in cases of renal parenchymal disease will increase. As the ability of the kidney to function decreases, it accepts less blood volume for filtration. The parenchymal resistance to flow increases and the resistive index value rises above 0.7.

column1:
What blood vessels are located at letter A?

A: 2 PTA and 1 PTV
B: 1 PTA and 2 PTV
C: 2 ATA and 1 ATV
D: 1 ATA and 2 ATV
column2:
Answer: D. 1 ATA and 2 ATV

Feedback:

column1:
Which of the following is most suggestive of a subclavian steal?

A: amaurosis fugax
B: systemic HTN
C: difference in brachial pressures >20 mmHg
D: congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: C. difference in brachial pressures >20 mmHg

Feedback:
Amaurosis fugax is usually related to ICA stenosis. HTN is a non-specific symptom and not a complication of a subclavian steal. Because the blood flow to the affected arm is being "pulled" from the brain, the pressure in the arm is much lower than the unaffected side. BP levels in both arms that differ more than 15mmHg are highly suspicious for the presence of a subclavian steal.

column1:
Which of the following would lead to overestimation of stenosis in the internal carotid artery?

A: Significant aortic valve stenosis
B: Tandem CCA stenosis
C: Compensatory flow due to stenosis on other side
D: Reduced systemic blood pressure
column2:
Answer: C. Compensatory flow due to stenosis on other side

Feedback:
Overestimation of stenosis in the ICA can be seen with: Systemic HTN Compensatory flow due to stenosis on other side Short area of focal stenosis Smaller vessel size Elevated heart rate Measuring a compensatory heartbeat with arrhythmias Lower systemic BP, aortic valve stenosis and tandem CCA stenosis would all slow the flow into the ICA stenosis and reduce the velocities in the ipsilateral ICA and underestimate ICA stenosis.

column1:
The brightness of the reflection on the Doppler tracing represents __________________.

A: the spatial pulse length of the reflection
B: the time duration of the reflection
C: the frequency of the reflection
D: the number of red blood cells moving at each velocity displayed
column2:
Answer: D. the number of red blood cells moving at each velocity displayed

Feedback:
The brightness of the reflection on the Doppler tracing represents the number of red blood cells moving at each velocity displayed. It is directly related to the signal strength.

column1:
While performing a lower extremity venous exam, you identify a small pair of veins with an accompanying artery deep in the calf muscle, that connect to the popliteal vein. What veins are these?

A: posterior arch veins
B: veins of Giacomini
C: sural veins
D: anterior tibial veins
column2:
Answer: C. sural veins

Feedback:
Sural veins are also called the gastrocnemius veins. They are located deep in the calf muscle and course alongside their accompanying artery. The sural veins empty their blood into the popliteal vein.

column1:
According to the Appropriate Use Criteria, which of the following is an appropriate indication for a renal artery duplex exam?

A: Peripheral arterial disease with well controlled hypertension
B: Hypertension in a young person (age <35 years)
C: Unilateral renal enlargement
D: Bilateral renal enlargement
column2:
Answer: B. Hypertension in a young person (age <35 years)

Feedback:
Uncontrollable or excessively high blood pressure (>160/80mmHg) are common indications for a renal artery duplex because the kidneys can raise the BP is their arterial flow is limited by stenosis. Hypertension in younger patients can be a sign of RAS, usually due to fibromuscular dysplasia. Discrepancy in kidney size can be a sign of RAS. Chronic stenosis can cause atrophy of the kidney that has limited arterial inflow. RAS can be the cause for kidneys that differ in length by more than 2cm. ***The appropriate use criteria offers common sense reasons for ordering exams. When evaluating these questions, consider the effectiveness of the exam in providing a diagnosis. We do not recommend that you attempt to memorize all indications.

column1:
Branches of the ___________________ connect to branches of the vertebral arteries to allow collateral flow from the anterior circulation to the posterior circulation.

A: occipital artery
B: ophthalmic artery
C: subclavian artery
D: basilar artery
column2:
Answer: A. occipital artery

Feedback:
The occipital artery is a branch of the ECA. Branches of the occipital artery meet branches of the vertebral artery. This is a possible collateral pathway for obstruction in the vertebral system or the ICA system on the same side. If vertebral branches meet basilar branches, this would be posterior circulation connected to posterior circulation. The ophthalmic artery is in the anterior aspect of the brain. Branching would not meet those of the posterior brain. The subclavian artery is in the upper chest.

column1:
A patient presents for follow up after a mesenteric stent placement procedure for a 75% SMA stenosis. Which of the following describes the findings in the SMA that indicate a successful procedure?

A: Increased resistance and decreased diastolic flow
B: Change from monophasic to peaked triphasic
C: Change from peaked triphasic to monophasic
D: Decreased resistance and increased diastolic flow
column2:
Answer: A. Increased resistance and decreased diastolic flow

Feedback:
Normal pre-prandial flow in the SMA is high resistance with low levels of diastolic flow (monophasic). 75% SMA stenosis would cause a low resistance waveform with increased systolic and diastolic velocities. The resistance would increase in the mesenteric vascular beds as normal flow levels resume after angioplasty. Diastolic flow levels would reduce because the mesentery is now receiving enough blood during systole. Peak systolic and end diastolic velocities are reduced.

column1:
Sclerotherapy:

A: is used to treat incompetent deep veins
B: involves the injection of saline into superficial varicosities
C: refers to the treatment of veins using a radiofrequency catheter
D: refers to the treatment of deep venous reflux with compression stockings
column2:
Answer: B. involves the injection of saline into superficial varicosities

Feedback:
Sclerotherapy refers to the injection of saline or other sclerosing agent that damages the vein and causes fibrosis to occur. Ablation can be used to avoid surgical intervention in some cases, catheter-based radiofrequency application can occlude the vessels leading to overall atrophy of the vessels.

column1:
Which of the following will not demonstrate a bruit on auscultation?

A: AV fistula
B: occlusion
C: stenosis
D: pseudoaneurysm
column2:
Answer: B. occlusion

Feedback:
An occlusion will not demonstrate a bruit because no flow is moving under the stethoscope.

column1:
Which of the following techniques can be used to assess the presence and timing of reflux in multiple vessels simultaneously?

A: PW Doppler
B: CW Doppler
C: color Doppler
D: Real time 2D imaging
column2:
Answer: C. color Doppler

Feedback:
A transverse view can demonstrate the GSV and one of its tributaries. Color Doppler with augmentation can demonstrate the presence and timing of reflux in both veins. This is very helpful in identifying recirculating reflux.

column1:
What Sonographic landmark is used to identify the distal end of the common iliac artery?

A: head of the quadricep muscle
B: the bifurcation into the external and internal iliac artery
C: the inguinal ligament
D: the saphenofemoral junction
column2:
Answer: B. the bifurcation into the external and internal iliac artery

Feedback:
The common iliac artery ends at the bifurcation into the external and internal iliac arteries. When the external iliac artery passes beneath the inguinal ligament, it becomes the common femoral artery. The inguinal ligament is an anatomic landmark for the external iliac artery, but it cannot normally be visualized when evaluating the extremity arteries.

column1:
Which of the following will lead to more color displayed in real time in the abdominal aorta?

A: increase sample size
B: increase persistence
C: decrease color threshold
D: decrease color gain
column2:
Answer: B. increase persistence

Feedback:
Persistence settings determine how much of the previous image data is carried over into the current image frame. Increasing this setting makes the live image appear smoother and with improved color display.

column1:
The supraorbital artery is a branch of the ___________________________. Branches of the supraorbital artery connect with branches of the _________________________, which is a branch of the ECA. This is a common collateral pathway from the ECA to the ICA.

A: ophthalmic artery, maxillary artery
B: ICA, facial artery
C: ICA, ophthalmic artery
D: ophthalmic artery, superficial temporal artery
column2:
Answer: D. ophthalmic artery, superficial temporal artery

Feedback:
The supraorbital artery is a branch of the ophthalmic artery. Branches for the supraorbital artery connect with branches of the superficial temporal artery, which is a branch of the ECA. This is a common collateral pathway from the ECA to the ICA.

column1:
If you detect flow toward the transducer in the right ACA while using the temporal window,

A: there is probably an occlusion of the left ICA
B: there is probably a left MCA occlusion
C: the flow direction is normal
D: there is probably an occlusion of the right ICA
column2:
Answer: D. there is probably an occlusion of the right ICA

Feedback:
When evaluating the ACA flow from the transtemporal window, flow should be moving away from the transducer. If flow direction is reversed in one of the anterior cerebral arteries, the change can be caused by cross-over collateralization. An ICA occlusion should be suspected on the same side.

column1:
The parenchymal resistance ratio is calculated to assess perfusion of which of the following organs?

A: liver
B: brain
C: kidney
D: muscles
column2:
Answer: C. kidney

Feedback:
Parenchymal resistance ratio = EDV / PSV Used to evaluate the parenchymal resistance in a transplant kidney

column1:
If a renal artery Doppler exam demonstrates stenosis but normal flow is identified on angiography evaluation, the US results are described as:

A: false positive
B: false negative
C: true positive
D: true negative
column2:
Answer: A. false positive

Feedback:
If the current exam is negative but the gold standard is positive = false negative results on current exam If the current exam is positive but the gold standard is negative= false positive results on current exam If the current exam is negative and the gold standard is negative = true negative results on current exam If the current exam is positive and the gold standard is positive = true positive results on current exam

column1:
Which drawing demonstrates an abnormal result for a venous photoplethysmography exam?

A: A
B: B
C: Both are abnormal responses
D: Unable to determine without the application of a tourniquet on the thigh
column2:
Answer: B. B

Feedback:
The exam involves placing a light sensor on the distal calf. Multiple calf raises are performed to empty the blood from the calf. The venous refill time of the calf is measured. A venous refill time less than 20 seconds indicates venous reflux. Image B shows a VRT <20sec.

column1:
A patient presents for a mesenteric Doppler to rule out stenosis. They have a history of diabetes, HTN, pain and nausea post-prandially. Which of the following appointment times would be best for this exam to be scheduled?

A: 1pm, after a good fatty meal
B: 230pm because the patient prefers this time
C: 8am, NPO for 6-8hrs prior
D: 10am, after light breakfast
column2:
Answer: C. 8am, NPO for 6-8hrs prior

Feedback:
Diabetic patients and those for studies that require an NPO status should be scheduled very early in the morning.

column1:
A diabetic patient presents for a segmental pressure exam. The patient complains of an injury to his big toe that won't seem to heal and has been open for over 3 weeks. The digital pressure in the affected toe will most likely be _________.

A: 30-60mmHg
B: 60-90mmHg
C: <30mmHg
D: >200mmHg
column2:
Answer: C. <30mmHg

Feedback:
Toe pressures <30mmHg correlate with a non-healing wound.

column1:
What is the normal change in flow velocity in response to releasing the cuff during reactive hyperemia?

A: decrease to less than half of the resting flow velocity
B: increase to more than double the resting flow velocity
C: increase by more than 35% above the resting flow velocity
D: decrease by more than 35% below the resting flow velocity
column2:
Answer: B. increase to more than double the resting flow velocity

Feedback:
The normal initial flow response to reactive hyperemia should include a flow velocity that is more than double the resting flow velocity. The body will try to make up for the loss of inflow during the cuff inflation as quickly as possible. The faster the recovery time, the more normal the exam. In a normal patient, the ankle pressures should drop by less than 35% and return to baseline levels in less than a minute.

column1:
What is the best Sonographic technique to use to evaluate the status of a pseudoaneurysm after 10 minutes of compression therapy is applied?

A: CW Doppler
B: Tissue Doppler
C: Color Doppler
D: PW Doppler
column2:
Answer: C. Color Doppler

Feedback:
Color Doppler is the best for identifying the presence of the stalk within the tissues. PW and CW Doppler can be used to evaluate the stalk once it has been located with color but cannot reliably locate the stalk without color guidance.

column1:
The 2D images displayed are from a normal renal Doppler evaluation in a patient with a history of 4.5cm distal AAA and acute onset of moderate HTN. What should you do to further evaluate or communicate the findings?

A: Include notes in the renal artery Doppler report for the reading physician regarding the presence of free fluid around the kidney that is most likely due to chronic renal failure.
B: Review the chart to confirm the suspected abnormal renal function testing results.
C: Include notes regarding the presence of ascites in the peritoneal cavity in the renal artery Doppler report for the reading physician.
D: Evaluate the AAA for change in size or wall continuity.
column2:
Answer: D. Evaluate the AAA for change in size or wall continuity.

Feedback:
The free fluid found in the perirenal space posterior to the kidney (not in peritoneal cavity) can indicate a rupture of the AAA which is the number one complication of AAA. The acute change in blood pressure also indicates a leak because the kidneys are raising the BP due to ischemia. The kidney demonstrates normal size and echotexture with no sign of renal failure.

column1:
Endovenous heat-induced thrombosis is a complication of:

A: microphlebectomy
B: chemical ablation
C: sclerotherapy
D: radiofrequency ablation
column2:
Answer: D. radiofrequency ablation

Feedback:
Endovenous heat-induced thrombosis (EHIT) is a complication of radiofrequency ablation. The thrombus that forms in the treated vein can extend into unintended vessels in the deep system. About 7 days after the procedure the patient should return to be evaluated for procedural success and complications like DVT or EHIT.

column1:
What is the cause of the vertical acoustic shadow seen on the images?

A: patient motion
B: electrical interference
C: element damage
D: air in the trachea
column2:
Answer: C. element damage

Feedback:
Note the vertical black stripe that originates at the face of the transducer. It is located in the same place on the image as you scan different anatomy.

column1:
The attached image is taken from a lower extremity venous Doppler exam. Which of the following should be included in your preliminary report?

A: The left PTV demonstrates abnormal absence of spontaneous flow but normal response to proximal augmentation.
B: The left PTV demonstrates normal flow and response to distal augmentation.
C: The left PTV demonstrates abnormal absence of spontaneous flow but normal response to distal augmentation.
D: The left PTV demonstrates normal flow and response to proximal augmentation.
column2:
Answer: B. The left PTV demonstrates normal flow and response to distal augmentation.

Feedback:
The deep calf veins and superficial veins do not normally exhibit spontaneous flow patterns. Augmentation is usually necessary to demonstrate patency of these veins.

column1:
An upper extremity segmental pressure exam produces the following pressures: Brachial RT 140mmHg AND LT 132mmHg Radial RT 144mmHg AND LT 136mmHg Ulnar RT 138mmHg AND LT 138mmHg 2nd Digit RT 130mmHg AND LT 104mmHg Which of the following correctly describes how to report the findings?

A: Suspected disease of the left palmar arch or digital artery of the second digit
B: Suspected disease of the right palmar arch or digital artery of the second digit
C: Suspected disease of the proximal left ulnar artery
D: Suspected disease of the proximal left radial artery
column2:
Answer: A. Suspected disease of the left palmar arch or digital artery of the second digit

Feedback:
When the arm pressures are normal but the digital pressure is abnormally reduced, there could be disease in the palmar arch or the single digit. To provide a complete evaluation, at least one more digit on the left hand should be evaluated. If the additional digit pressure is abnormal, disease in the palmar arch is the most likely cause for the reduced digit pressures. If the additional digit pressure is normal, the remaining digits should be tested to confirm that the reduced pressure is limited to the single digit and disease of that digital artery should be suspected.

column1:
What lab values may be elevated with renal artery stenosis?

A: hematocrit
B: blood pressure
C: prothrombin time
D: serum BUN and creatinine
column2:
Answer: D. serum BUN and creatinine

Feedback:
Renal artery stenosis will limit the blood flow through the kidney. This decrease in renal filtration of blood can be demonstrated in lab testing by increased serum levels of BUN and creatinine. Systemic BP will rise with significant RAS, but it is not a lab value.

column1:
Which of the following describes the expected change in the PVR tracing on an immediate post procedure evaluation of a patient after successful revascularization of the common femoral artery?

A: loss of the dicrotic notch
B: increased amplitude by >100%
C: decreased amplitude by >50%
D: increased amplitude by >50%
column2:
Answer: D. increased amplitude by >50%

Feedback:
Normal PVR tracings from an extremity should be high amplitude with a dicrotic notch. If the revascularization was a success, the amplitude of the waveform should have increased at least 50% compared to the amplitude on the pre-op exam.

column1:
Which of the following has the greatest effect on resistance to blood flow?

A: Two different arteries with 60% stenosis supplying the same organ
B: A 60% stenosis in a single artery
C: Two consecutive areas of 60% stenosis in a single artery
D: All scenarios above would have the same variation in resistance
column2:
Answer: C. Two consecutive areas of 60% stenosis in a single artery

Feedback:
Stenoses in a series - multiple areas of stenosis in the same vessel will increase the resistance to flow more than a single stenotic area; velocity and stenosis can be underestimated at the second stenosis. Stenoses in parallel - stenosis in different vessels coursing in the same direction (collaterals); has a lesser effect on resistance to flow than series stenoses

column1:
You are about to perform a lower extremity arterial duplex on a patient that recently had an MRA run-off exam. According to the attached image, what vessel will demonstrate reduced or absent flow in the left leg?

A: common femoral artery
B: superficial femoral artery
C: posterior tibial artery
D: anterior tibial artery
column2:
Answer: D. anterior tibial artery

Feedback:
Note the limited display of contrast in the superficial femoral artery of the right leg and the anterior tibial artery of the left leg. You should expect that Doppler evaluation of these vessels will demonstrate little to no flow.

column1:
A patient is referred for a Doppler ultrasound of the IVC and hepatic veins due to an abnormal echocardiogram. The report states that the hepatic veins appear to empty directly into the right atrium with limited visualization of the upper IVC. What are you looking for in the abdomen?

A: duplicated IVC
B: azygous or hemiazygos IVC
C: situs inversus
D: portal HTN
column2:
Answer: B. azygous or hemiazygos IVC

Feedback:
Interrupted IVC - failure of the IVC to form correctly in the upper abdomen/liver; azygous and hemiazygos veins drain blood from the distal IVC segment; hepatic veins drain directly into the right atrium.

column1:
Which of the following statements correctly describes an endovenous laser ablation of the GSV?

A: The procedure is considered a success if thrombus fills the GSV from the ankle to the SFJ
B: Intravascular ultrasound is performed after the procedure to confirm success
C: The physician injects a halo of anesthesia medicine around the GSV
D: The procedure is much more easily performed if the patient already has DVT
column2:
Answer: C. The physician injects a halo of anesthesia medicine around the GSV

Feedback:
Physician injects anesthesia around the vein being treated = tumescent anesthesia. A 1cm ring of anesthesia medicine should surround the vein for the appropriate level of patient comfort during the procedure. The catheter is inserted in the distal vein and advanced to the most proximal point that will be treated. The laser is turned on and the physician slowly retracts the catheter until it reaches the insertion point. A follow up ultrasound exam is performed in a week or two to evaluate the success of the procedure. The deep system should be evaluated for thrombus, the treated vein should be evaluated for diameter measurements and with color flow. The distance between the SFJ and most proximal point of closure of the GSV should be measured. If thrombus extends into the deep system, the GSV must be ligated. DVT is a contraindication for the procedure.

column1:
In order to assess low velocity flow, which of the following operator adjustments should be made?

A: decrease wall filter setting and compression
B: increase scale setting
C: decrease compression
D: decrease the rejection and wall filter setting
column2:
Answer: D. decrease the rejection and wall filter setting

Feedback:
Decreasing the wall filter and rejection settings will increase the amount of low-level Doppler shifts displayed on the tracing. Venous flow is low velocity and demonstrates low level Doppler shifts.

column1:
Which of the following transducers would be preferred for a lower extremity DVT evaluation on an average sized patient?

A: 7-10MHz sector
B: 5-7MHz linear
C: 10-12MHz linear
D: 5-7MHz curvilinear
column2:
Answer: B. 5-7MHz linear

Feedback:
Linear arrays are preferred for vascular exams. 5-7MHz transducers are used for DVT evaluation in an average sized patient. Higher frequencies can be used for evaluation of the superficial system.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes a pre-procedure vein mapping?

A: The diameter of the vein is usually written on the arm/leg at the location it was taken
B: Veins should maintain a uniform elliptical shape during measurements
C: Keep the transducer pressure constant at a medium level
D: Larger amounts of gel will aid in visualization of the superficial vessels
column2:
Answer: A. The diameter of the vein is usually written on the arm/leg at the location it was taken

Feedback:
To protect the transducer from staining from the marker, cover it with a nonsterile probe cover. Remember to use limited gel to allow for easier and more permanent skin marking. The marks need to remain until the procedure is performed. A permanent marker or carbol fuchsin stain (used in radiation therapy) is used to create the markings. Keep the transducer perpendicular to the skin surface and use very light transducer pressure. If the vein appears elliptical, then too much transducer pressure is being applied or the transducer is off axis. A small mark is placed in front of the ultrasound transducer along the narrow edge of the transducer. If a transverse approach is used, the vein should appear circular and centered on the ultrasound screen. A skin mark is then placed exactly at the center of the long face of the transducer. Once the initial skin mark is placed, the transducer is moved slightly distally toward the foot while keeping the rounded vein in the center of view. A new mark is placed on the skin every 2 to 3 cm. A straw could be used to make an indention in the skin and then these marks are connected by lines drawn with a permanent marker after the gel is removed. The diameter of the vein is usually written on the arm/leg at the location it was taken.

column1:
Which of the following statements best describes the reason why there is an absence of color flow in the vessel on the image?

A: The ultrasound beam is perpendicular to the motion of the blood, so no flow is detected.
B: The ultrasound beam is parallel to the motion of the blood, so no flow is detected.
C: There is an occlusion present. Flow is demonstrated proximal to the lesion with collateral flow filling the distal portion of the vessel.
D: The color Doppler gain is set too low to detect the slower flow at the curve in the vessel.
column2:
Answer: A. The ultrasound beam is perpendicular to the motion of the blood, so no flow is detected.

Feedback:
2D imaging relies on perpendicular incidence of the beam for the best image. Doppler evaluation detects blood moving parallel to the axis of the beam. If flow is moving perpendicular to the beam (90 degrees), no flow will be detected.

column1:
Which of the following exams is typically performed without angle correction on Doppler evaluation?

A: renal artery duplex
B: aorta duplex
C: TIPS evaluation
D: IVC duplex
column2:
Answer: D. IVC duplex

Feedback:
Any Doppler exam that requires measurement of blood flow velocity requires angle correction to obtain an accurate velocity. This includes all arterial exams and TIPS evaluations. A duplex exam of the IVC is used to assess patency and flow variation with respiration and cardiac pulsatility. No velocity information is used in the IVC duplex exam.

column1:
The best obtainable angle of insonation for vascular evaluation that is most commonly used to provide Doppler shift information is_________degrees.

A: 0
B: 45
C: 60
D: 90
column2:
Answer: C. 60

Feedback:
The best angle of insonation to produce the most accurate Doppler shift information is 0 degrees. The best OBTAINABLE angle of insonation of a blood vessel that is most commonly used to provide Doppler shift information is 60 degrees.

column1:
The Doppler tracing demonstrates electrical noise. How can you reduce or eliminate the artifact?

A: Increase the wall filter settings
B: Extend the cord to move the machine as far from the electrical outlet as possible
C: Decrease the PRF
D: Decrease wall filter settings
column2:
Answer: A. Increase the wall filter settings

Feedback:
Noise can be reduced by decreasing Doppler gain, increasing wall filter settings, or trying another wall outlet.

column1:
What blood flow characteristic is being measured on the Doppler tracings provided?

A: Resistive index
B: Pulsatility index
C: Acceleration time
D: Peak systolic velocity
column2:
Answer: C. Acceleration time

Feedback:
Acceleration time refers to the time from the onset of systole to the peak systolic velocity. The higher the resistance, the shorter the AT.

column1:
When wearing a protective gown and gloves:

A: the open end of the gloves should be fitted under the wrist cuffs of the gown
B: two pairs of gloves should be worn
C: the open end of the gloves should be pulled over the wrist cuffs of the gown
D: the open end of the gloves should not be touching the wrist cuffs of the gown
column2:
Answer: C. the open end of the gloves should be pulled over the wrist cuffs of the gown

Feedback:
When wearing a protective gown and gloves the open end of the gloves should be pulled over the wrist cuffs of the gown. The hands and arms are then fully protected.

column1:
_____________________ is the gold standard in testing for arterial disease.

A: Segmental Pressures with Duplex US
B: Angiography
C: Angioplasty
D: Venography
column2:
Answer: B. Angiography

Feedback:
Arteriography is the most sensitive and specific test for assessment of arterial disease, but many complications reduce its application. Aside from the issue of radiation exposure, the invasive nature causes increased risk of infection, abscess or pseudoaneurysm formation. The iodine-based contrast is also not tolerated by all patients.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the sensitivity of a testing technique?

A: The ability of a test to detect disease when it IS present.
B: If the test result is positive, sensitivity is the probability that the patient actually has the disease
C: The ability of a test to rule out disease when it IS NOT present.
D: If the test result is negative sensitivity is the probability that the patient does not have disease
column2:
Answer: A. The ability of a test to detect disease when it IS present.

Feedback:
Sensitivity-The ability of a test to detect disease when it IS present. The number of truly positive tests performed in the current modality is compared to the positive tests diagnosed by the gold standard test.

column1:
Which of the following should be increased when evaluating venous flow with color Doppler?

A: color priority
B: PRF
C: wall filter
D: output power
column2:
Answer: A. color priority

Feedback:
The threshold (priority) control is used to adjust the appearance of color Doppler. Increased threshold = increased color display. Increasing the wall filter or the PRF will reduce the appearance of lower velocity flow. Changes to the power output on the system will increase the beam intensity and penetration.
